skill.name: coding-agent-pm
skill.description: Plan, assign, review, and verify coding-agent work with tight scope control and clear acceptance criteria.
skill.summary: Lightweight PM operating loop for managing Claude Code, Codex, and other coding agents — brief, delegate, review, and ship with clear scope and acceptance criteria.
skill.author: Bridge AI Labs

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged 61 potential issues, but all are false positives on manual review. The skill contains only Markdown documentation and templates — no executable code. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are Markdown code fences (```), the 'weak cryptographic algorithm' matches are review checklist text about security, the 'hidden file in home directory' matches are install path instructions (~/.claude/skills/), and hardcoded URLs are the project's GitHub and Gumroad pages. No malicious intent or harmful behavior was found.

content.user_title: Manage Coding Agents With Clear Scope and Acceptance Criteria
content.value_statement: Coding agents drift, expand scope, and skip verification — this skill provides a lightweight PM operating loop with templates for briefing, reviewing, and shipping their work. It gives founders and product engineers a repeatable system to delegate, review, and verify agent output without losing control.
actual_capabilities: Define a six-step operating loop for assigning and reviewing coding-agent work
Generate a structured PM agent brief with outcome, scope, context, and acceptance criteria
Create task plans with ownership boundaries, forbidden actions, and verification steps
Provide reusable prompt snippets for planning, implementation, review, drift recovery, and delegation
Supply a review checklist covering scope, behavior, code quality, verification, and security/privacy
Track first-week agent workflow results with a scoring rubric for time saved and rework
limitations: Does not execute or run coding agents — it is a documentation and template skill only
Does not provide automated code review — it gives human reviewers a structured checklist
Does not integrate with any external project management or version control tools
Requires the user to manually fill in project-specific details in all templates

use_cases.title: Delegate a feature task to a coding agent
use_cases.description: A founder uses the PM agent brief template to define outcome, scope, and acceptance criteria before assigning a feature to Claude Code or Codex.
use_cases.target_user: Founders and solo operators using AI coding agents

use_cases.title: Review agent-submitted pull requests systematically
use_cases.description: A product engineer applies the review checklist to evaluate an agent's diff for scope drift, missing tests, security issues, and verification quality.
use_cases.target_user: Product engineers and tech leads managing AI-generated code

use_cases.title: Recover when a coding agent drifts off-scope
use_cases.description: A PM uses the drift-recovery prompt snippet to stop the agent, map changes back to acceptance criteria, and re-establish boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Create a PM agent brief
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pm-agent-brief template. Fill in: (1) the one-sentence shipped outcome, (2) in-scope and out-of-scope files and behaviors, (3) project context and conventions, (4) acceptance criteria that can be checked directly, (5) review questions about scope drift and secret exposure.
prompt_templates.scenario: Preparing a new task for delegation to a coding agent

prompt_templates.title: Plan a coding task with boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the task-plan-template. Define project name, goal, owner, current state, target state, and the work plan steps. Then fill in the agent assignment prompt with owned files, excluded files, acceptance criteria, and validation commands.

prompt_templates.title: Review agent output with a checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the review-checklist template. Walk through scope, behavior, code quality, verification, and security/privacy sections. For each unchecked item, note whether the agent's final response addressed it or not.
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluating a completed coding-agent task before shipping

prompt_templates.title: Recover from scope drift and delegate cleanly
prompt_templates.prompt: First, use the recovery prompt snippet: tell the agent to stop, return to the original acceptance criteria, and list any out-of-scope changes. Then, use the delegation snippet to split remaining work into independent tasks with disjoint write scopes, each with owned files, expected output, and validation commands.
prompt_templates.scenario: Re-establishing control when an agent has expanded scope

output_examples.input: User asks: 'I need to assign a bug fix to Claude Code. Help me prepare a brief.'
output: Outcome: Fix the login redirect loop so users land on /dashboard after sign-in.
Scope (in): auth/session.ts, auth/redirect.ts, relevant test files
Scope (out): no refactors, no UI changes, no dependency upgrades
Context: Next.js 14 app with NextAuth. Redirect configured in middleware.ts.
Acceptance criteria: (1) sign-in redirects to /dashboard, (2) existing tests pass, (3) no console errors, (4) final response lists changed files and test results
Review questions: Did the agent touch files outside auth/? Did it expose session tokens in logs?

output_examples.input: User asks: 'The agent just finished a task. How should I review it?'
output: Scope: Confirm changed files match the assignment; no unrelated refactors or reverted work.
Behavior: Check acceptance criteria are met, edge cases handled, error states deliberate.
Verification: Confirm relevant tests ran; manual checks done if tests are insufficient; failures reported honestly.
Security/Privacy: No secrets committed or printed; no private data in logs; no destructive command without approval.

best_practices: Write the shipped outcome as a single concrete sentence before assigning any work
State explicitly which files the agent owns and which it must not touch
Require the agent to report changed files, commands run, results, and remaining risks in its final response
Review the diff before accepting work, prioritizing behavioral bugs and security issues over style
anti_patterns: Assigning a vague task without scope boundaries or acceptance criteria
Accepting agent output without inspecting the diff or running validation
Letting the agent revert unrelated user work or invent new abstractions when existing patterns suffice

faq.question: What AI tools does this skill work with?
faq.answer: It works with Claude Code, Codex, OpenClaw, and any other coding agent where a human retains scope, review, and shipping decisions.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code or run agents?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation and template skill. It provides the operating loop, prompts, and checklists you use when working with coding agents.

faq.question: What is the PM operating loop?
faq.answer: Six steps: define the outcome, set scope, capture context, write acceptance criteria, require validation, and review the diff.

faq.question: Can I customize the templates for my project?
faq.answer: Yes. All templates include bracketed placeholders. Replace them with project-specific files, commands, and criteria before assigning work.

faq.question: How is this different from a generic prompt library?
faq.answer: It bundles an operating loop, a brief template, a task plan, a review checklist, a drift-recovery prompt, and a tracking sheet into one system focused on scope control and verification.

faq.question: Is this skill free to use?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is released under the MIT license. A packaged version is also available free on Gumroad via the link in the README.
